title: Engineering Services

labor_share.derivation: BLS Industry Productivity via FRED — Labor Compensation (IPUMN541330L020000000) $172,759.143M / Sectoral Output (IPUMN541330T300000000) $367,702.147M for NAICS 541330, 2024 = 0.470. Fixture note: carried from the v2 prototype run; in production this value comes from pipeline/datasets/.

role_mix.derivation: BLS OEWS May 2023 NAICS 541330 occupation table (https://www.bls.gov/oes/2023/may/naics5_541330.htm), shares of labor cost. Fixture note: carried from the v2 prototype run.

n_total.derivation: naicslist.com / Census-derived (https://naicslist.com/naics/541330): 38,516 companies active in NAICS 541330, est. employment 1,098,479. Fixture note: production value comes from pipeline/datasets/ (SUSB).

n_band.derivation: Zweig Group 2024 median firm: 38 FTE, NSR $6.3M, EBITDA $965K (~15.3% margin); $1M EBITDA ≈ NSR $6.5M, $10M EBITDA ≈ NSR $65M. SUSB size distribution: ~50% of 38,516 firms are <10 employees (below $1M EBITDA); ~30% (~11,555 firms) in 10-249 employee range; ~60% of those below $10M EBITDA → 11,555 × 0.6 ≈ 6,933, rounded to 7,000. ESTIMATE — SUSB 6-digit breakdown not directly fetched.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Engineer-of-record stamp, liability, client trust are non-automatable; analysis and report-writing hours are heavily AI-exposed. 25% fraction conservative for report drafting, literature review, calculations review.
Calculations, modeling and report drafting automate substantially; field verification and judgment calls persist. 50% fraction covers document production and routine analysis.
Lab/materials testing and data collection partly physical; data reduction and report prep automate. 35% fraction.
Production drawings are the most automatable cost line; generative AI tools already produce draft sets. 25% retained for quality review, jurisdictional compliance, and revision cycles.
Client/agency coordination persists; schedule, budget and submittal paperwork automates. 30% fraction.
Physical site presence (construction inspection, surveying) — AI-resistant, drone-augmented only at margins. 5% for data processing back in office.
Proposal production for QBS pursuits is a large, automatable overhead. 65% for drafting, formatting, boilerplate. 35% retained for strategic pursuits and relationship-based writing.

pi_dist.rationale: Public-sector clients (DOTs, municipalities; ~50% of revenue) select via QBS on on-call/MSA contracts held by the firm, but re-competed every 3-5 years; individual principals matter for retention. Private/developer clients (~30%) hold closer relationships at firm level. Industrial (~15%) are more personal. FAR cost-plus audited overhead means AI efficiency gains on public work reduce billable cost and are passed to clients rather than retained as margin — a direct pi_dist depressant. Engineer-of-record is institutional to the firm, not entirely personal. Net score: 0.55 reflecting partial firm-level control diluted by QBS re-competition and cost-plus billing for public share.
sources: FAQs: Overhead Rate Audits & FAR Part 31 for A&E Firms — Cherry Bekaert (https://www.cbh.com/insights/articles/faqs-overhead-rate-audits-far-part-31-for-ae-firms/)

pi_moat.rationale: PE licensure (engineer-of-record stamp) creates legal barrier — every state requires a PE in responsible charge, blocking AI-only bypass for any stamped deliverable. Firm registration requirements add layer. However: (1) FAR/AASHTO overhead audits reduce AI-driven cost advantages for public work (clawback channel); (2) QBS re-competition every 3-5 years limits lock-in; (3) developer/private clients can adopt plan-stamping (insource with AI + single stamping PE) though responsible-charge rules constrain spread; (4) proprietary project data and records create moderate switching cost. Net: licensure provides genuine but imperfect moat; pricing structure deflates margin capture. Score 0.55.
sources: FAR Part 31 overhead audit structure — Cherry Bekaert, GMA CPA (https://www.gma-cpa.com/blog/far-overhead-rates-and-audits-for-a/e-firms-faqs)

t50_years.derivation: Current material adoption ~32% (mid-2026). Historical analogues (CAD, BIM) took 8-10 years total to 50%. AI adoption has been tripling YoY (Deltek). Starting from near-zero ~2022, 4-year S-curve would put 50% around 2026-2028. Given current 32% and tripling YoY trend slowing to 1.5-2x per year as diffusion broadens, estimate 3 more years (2029) to reach 50% material adoption in automatable functions. t50 = 3 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: Deltek Clarity 46th Annual A&E Industry Study (2025)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 53% of firms already use AI and machine learning; two-thirds have embraced generative AI. Narrowed to material use in automatable production functions (not just experimentation) from 53% headline to ~32% — proposal-level AI is not the same as production-workflow AI.

historical_analogs.value: CAD adoption from manual drafting: ~10 years to reach 50% (early 1980s to early 1990s). BIM adoption: ~8-10 years from early adopters (~2004) to ~50% (~2013-2015). Both analogues involve similar professional inertia, licensure-related caution, and capital investment.
historical_analogs.source: Industry knowledge; no single primary source for analog comparison. Secondary: AIA and ENR historical adoption curves discussed in trade literature.
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: CAD ~10 years to 50%; BIM ~8-10 years to ~50%

owners_60plus_pct.source: Zweig Group 2024 Valuation Report of AEC Firms; ACEC Research Institute OTMS survey and workforce analysis
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 'nearly 80 percent of firm owners looking to transition in the next decade' — implies large share aged 55+; 45% aged 60+ is a conservative estimate consistent with documented succession shortage.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Zweig Group 2024 Valuation Report: 'nearly 80 percent of firm owners looking to transition in the next decade'; ACEC OTMS survey confirms aging ownership base and thin internal-transition pipeline.

active_consolidators.source: Morrissey Goodale 2024 M&A data; Auxo Capital 2025 data
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 25+ PE-backed platforms named (Salas O'Brien, Verdantas, WSB, IMEG, Ardurra, Consor, WGI/First Reserve, CPL/GHK Capital, RTM/MML Capital, Olsson/MSCP, Bowman/Nasdaq, NV5/Nasdaq, Pennoni, Mainstay, and 15+ others). PE-backed share of AE M&A exceeded 50% of all deals in 2024-2025. Counted conservatively at 30 active add-on platforms in last 24 months.

buy_mult.source: Zweig Group 2024 Valuation Report of AEC Firms; Auxo Capital 2026 guide
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Zweig median value/EBITDA = 4.23 (all-size survey); Auxo: smaller founder-led engineering firms 3.5x-5.5x adjusted EBITDA. Normalized buy multiple for single-location/$1-5M EBITDA firm: 4.5x (above Zweig median to account for EBITDA normalization per rule 3 — owner comp added back).

exit_mult.source: Auxo Capital Advisors 2026 guide: Engineering Firm Valuation Multiples
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Platform-quality engineering $10M+ EBITDA at 8.5-12x EBITDA; PE paying avg 10.6x for construction/engineering 2018-2025; WSP/TRC deal at 15.9x (strategic outlier). Conservative platform exit: 9.0x as midpoint of 8.5-10x for established multi-state platform (below strategic premium).

pricing_structure.value: ~40-55% hourly/cost-plus with FAR overhead multiplier (federal and state DOT work); ~10-15% QBS negotiated lump sum/hourly (municipal); ~30-40% fixed-fee/lump-sum (private developer and industrial clients).
pricing_structure.source: FAR Part 31 overhead audit literature — Cherry Bekaert; Deltek Clarity A&E Study
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Under cost-plus/FAR billing: AI reduces direct labor hours → reduces overhead base → reduces total billing → efficiency gain flows to client, not firm. Under fixed-fee billing the firm captures the margin. The ~50% public-sector cost-plus exposure is the primary deflation channel.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.177895 → theoretical EBITDA uplift of 17.79pp (gross). However, ~50% of AE revenue flows through FAR/AASHTO cost-plus audited contracts where AI-driven labor hour reductions reduce overhead recovery and billing directly — client captures efficiency gain, not the firm. Effective retained uplift for the operator ≈ 17.79 × 0.50 (private/fixed-fee share) = ~8.9pp. With A ≈ 3.3 (moderate-slow adoption), this is consistent: significant but partially captured value being unlocked gradually. No contradiction requiring revision.

terminal_value.justification: Near-term: PE stamp, EOR liability, and infrastructure-funding demand (IIJA/BIL) keep engineering services paid demand robust — AI cuts production hours, it does not replace the stamped deliverable. Medium-term: AI-collapsed production hours risk converting the industry into a smaller-headcount stamp-and-liability business, and on the dominant public cost-plus side FAR overhead audits pass AI productivity gains to clients as lower billing — demand persists but revenue per engagement shrinks. Classified PRESSURED (demand shrinks but persists) per the run's own words: 'terminal value ambiguous and dependent on client-mix evolution' — fixture-adaptation judgment call, see reviewer_note.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V derived from FRED BLS labor compensation/output ratio (primary source, no deal activity) and OEWS occupation breakdown (primary source). A derived from Deltek Clarity AI adoption survey of A&E firms (primary survey, no M&A reference). Neither V nor A was informed by PE deal count, consolidation waves, or acquisition multiples.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Material deflation channel confirmed. Billing mix per pricing_structure input: (1) hourly/cost-plus with FAR overhead multiplier ~40-55% of sector revenue; (2) QBS negotiated ~10-15%; (3) fixed-fee ~30-40%. Under cost-plus/FAR billing AI efficiency flows to the client, not the firm — structural mechanism confirmed by FAR Part 31 overhead audit literature. Under fixed-fee billing the firm captures the margin. Reflected in pi_dist = 0.55 and pi_moat = 0.55, which discount AI's value to operators specifically because of the pricing-model clawback on the public side.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 10/2 = 5.0 (t50=2) to 10/5 = 2.0 (t50=5); central estimate A = 3.33. S moves from 4.70 to ~5.10 (t50=2) or ~4.25 (t50=5). Largest single-input uncertainty.
TD = log10(n_band)/4 moves from 0.869 (3,000) to 1.0 (10,000); B moves from ~7.62 to ~8.17; S moves by roughly ∓0.05.
C ranges from 10×0.45×0.50 = 2.25 to 10×0.65×0.60 = 3.90; S moves from ~4.43 to ~4.95 — the upper edge stays below the strong cut (5.5), the lower edge stays above the conditional cut (4.0).

reviewer_note: REGRESSION FIXTURE — adapted 2026-07-20 from the v2-format prototype run (2026-07-20_sonnet_tv2.json) to the v3 schema; permanent build test per V3_PLAN 2.4. All scores recomputed with template-3.0 formulas (floats, no PD in B): V 7.1158 / C 3.025 / A 3.3333 / B 8.0121 / M 4.0, S 4.7025 (v2 integers were 7/3/3/6/4, product 504). Adaptation judgment calls: (1) terminal_value class set to PRESSURED from the v2 record's ambivalent terminal-value text ('demand is not being replaced, hours are being reduced' but 'terminal value ambiguous and dependent on client-mix evolution' with the cost-plus clawback channel) — not a fresh research finding; (2) succession_shortage_documented = true per the Zweig transition figure already cited in the v2 record; (3) access_date 2026-07-20 applied where the v2 record carried none. The dominant economic tension is unchanged from the v2 run: FAR/cost-plus pricing deflation suppresses C (3.025) despite high V (7.1158).
